title: @ Stuff - peer assessment
body:
I think that results for peer assessment on sample page are wrong. e.g. for the 200 period number of snow hare has to be 953.88 not 903.13. Using double precision variable type  on a loop result can't be 903.13. 
Jumping directly to 200 period result may be 903.13 snow hare but you can't do this because of ancestors. 
In first year your number of snow hare isn't 270 but is 207.00000002. So if you jump directly to 200 period you will not calculate even this .000000002. So period by period this .000000002 will increase in value and after 200 period number of snow hare will be 953.88 not 903.13.
I use same equation in a proper manner but I get different results just because I use a loop which calculate even this .00000002 that increase period by period.
I may publish here my source code if this helps.
So please take a look at this.
author: student1

comments.author: student1
student2
comments.body:
Hi,

@ugabrielu

I think that the results on the webpage are correct, mainly because I find exactly the same (except for the roundings, of course). And I am pretty sure that my code is correct, because I am a seasoned programmer, and I am used to reason about my code (and others' code). I posted my results in another thread, but I copy-paste them here again:

    Result after 1 period:
     - hares : 270,000000 (expected 270,000000)
     - lynxes: 19,920000 (expected 19,920000)
    
    Result after 2 periods:
     - hares : 243,216000 (expected 243,216000)
     - lynxes: 19,828368 (expected 19,828368)
    
    Result after 20 periods:
     - hares : 47,153503 (expected 47,150000)
     - lynxes: 17,286473 (expected 17,280000)
    
    Result after 100 periods:
     - hares : 8,444519 (expected 8,440000)
     - lynxes: 7,898029 (expected 7,890000)
    
    Result after 200 periods:
     - hares : 903,171262 (expected 903,170000)
     - lynxes: 3,915382 (expected 3,910000)

The format is

    - <animal>: <computed_value> (expected <webpage_value>)

As you can see, the computed values are the same as the expected values. My program uses double precision. The values are printed out on the screen using `System.out.format("... %f ...", value)`, hence the six decimals.

By the way, unless you are using another formula for the Lotka-Volterra model, I would think that if your code is too complicated, you are probably doing something wrong...

comments.author: student1
comments.body:
Hi Juan

I'm a pretty good programmer too, but this doesn't matter.
So, your result for first year isn't 270,000000, it has to be 270.00000000000006, my code get this result, for second year you have to use this 270.00000000000006 not 270,000000. For second year(period) you have to use 270.00000000000006 as argument in equation not 270,000000. My code gets for second year 243.21600000000007 not 243,216000 so for the next period 3, you have to use 243.21600000000007 as argument in equation, for next period result from precedent result will be argument for it and so on. Which is number of snow hare on 199 period that you use as argument to get number of snow hare and lynx in 200 period? But number of snow hares on 198 period to get the proper number of snow hares and lynxes that will be argument to find out number of snow hare and lynx , that will be argument for 199 period that is argument for 200 period and so on?  You can't give these numbers. You jumped directly to 200 period.
To find out the proper result for the 200 period you have to get something like this:
Result after 1 period:
 - hares : 270,000000 (expected 270,000000) //wrong value
 - lynxes: 19,920000 (expected 19,920000)

Result after 2 periods:
 - hares : 243,216000 (expected 243,216000) //wrong value
 - lynxes: 19,828368 (expected 19,828368)

.
.
.
Result after 199 periods:
 - hares : xxxx (expected xxxx)
 - lynxes: xxxxx (expected xxxxxx).

Result after 200 periods:
 - hares : 903,171262 (expected 903,170000) // wrong value
 - lynxes: 3,915382 (expected 3,910000)

comments.author: student1
student2
comments.body:
Ok, ugabrielu,

Here are my results without formatting the values:

    Result after 1 period:
     - hares : 270.00000000000006 (expected 270.0)
     - lynxes: 19.92 (expected 19.92)
    
    Result after 2 periods:
     - hares : 243.21600000000007 (expected 243.216)
     - lynxes: 19.828368 (expected 19.828368)
    
    Result after 20 periods:
     - hares : 47.15350338641354 (expected 47.15)
     - lynxes: 17.286472722798493 (expected 17.28)
    
    Result after 100 periods:
     - hares : 8.444518662594474 (expected 8.44)
     - lynxes: 7.898029484922736 (expected 7.89)
    
    Result after 200 periods:
     - hares : 903.1712618946665 (expected 903.17)
     - lynxes: 3.915382396023398 (expected 3.91)

As I was saying, my program uses the type `double` everywhere.

What I do in my program is something like:

    ... preyPredLV(..., int n) {
      ...
      for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        // do computations
      }
      ...
      return theFinalResult;
    }
    
    ... main(...) {
       ... 
       ... preyPredLV(..., 1);
       ... // print out results on screen.
       ... 
       ... preyPredLV(..., 2);
       ... // print out results on screen.
       ... 
       ... preyPredLV(..., 20);
       ... // print out results on screen.
       ... 
       ... preyPredLV(..., 100);
       ... // print out results on screen.
       ...
       ... preyPredLV(..., 200);
       ... // print out results on screen.
    }

So my results are computed with the correct values all the time. And what I print out on the screen is always the last value computed using double precision always.

And I get these results if you are interested:

    Result after 198 periods:
     - hares : 801.948029330715
     - lynxes: 3.864737465299042
    
    Result after 199 periods:
     - hares : 851.1496463220151
     - lynxes: 3.888076462529594

comments.author: student2
student1
comments.body:
@Juan

Yes, you computed correct but with wrong values. Please try to understand what I said. You can't find out proper result on this manner. Please use equation from web page and calculate one by one manually for each period. Use results from first period as argument for the second(manually), then results from second period as argument for third period(manually) and so on. For first 10 periods you will not get noticeable difference but after 10 period you will see that.
I can help you with these values:

comments.author: student3
comments.body:
@ugabrielu
You are not using correct formulas in your equations since in the particular generation you are using newly computed values for computing the next in the same generation. This is not how the equations in the model are defined. It is not about "reality", but how the model is created. So, even if you think (and I cant object it, since it is purely your personal thing) that model is wrong, the model is how it written on the website. Using those equations than leads to results same as on the website.

comments.author: student3
comments.body: No problem, pal. Wish you success on the rest of course.
